name: Phase 2 — Start Here (How to Run This)

text.content:
Phase 2 — The Dead Man's Receipts

A runnable chapter for Blooming Rot, Part 2. This page is the GM's orientation. Read it before opening any other Phase 2 journal.

“Haskur Vandrell did not flee to safety. He fled to an account someone had already opened for him.”

What this chapter is

The party went east of the Jotuns and broke a man named Haskur Vandrell — broke him badly, left him for finished — and then watched him walk away from a fight he had no business surviving. They came home to Loftwick to a hero's welcome they did not entirely trust, a quiet old spymaster named Caelith Dunivar who lodged them in the Little Palace and asked them to look at what Haskur left behind, and, at the very end of the evening, a single letter delivered by ordinary post, sealed with a crane in flight above three coins, that read: We hope your visit to Loftwick is brief.

This chapter is the morning after that letter. It is not a dungeon and it is barely a fight. It is an investigation that takes place almost entirely indoors and on foot, across three days in a single city, and its horror is administrative: forged paperwork, a quietly murdered clerk, a missing grain factor, a polite man with a bribe, and a professional thief sent for evidence rather than blood. By the end of it the party will understand that the man they beat was never the point — that he was a line item in somebody's ledger, pulled back from death twice in nine years because killing him was cheaper to undo than to allow — and they will leave Loftwick riding hard for the east, because every road they pull on runs the same way.

Run it warmly and run it slow. The pleasure of this chapter is watching intelligent players assemble a conspiracy out of receipts.

The shape of the chapter

Three movements, across roughly three in-fiction days and two to four sessions.

  • Movement One — The Table. The party comes down to breakfast and finds Haskur's possessions laid out as evidence in daylight; Caelith tells them who he really is and what he wants; and the morning's reading turns a handful of objects into three names and two streets to walk to.
  • Movement Two — The City. The party works two neighborhoods at their own pace. Wick Lane is the dense one — the firm's local office, the dead clerk's rooms, the physician's surgery, and a watcher posted on the corner who turns the street into a footchase. Tannerway is the human one — the missing woman's counting house, her husband's house, the watch sergeant who failed her. Whichever thread they pull, it points east; the quiet triumph of the chapter is the moment they lay the threads side by side and see the road for themselves.
  • Movement Three — The Squeeze and the Road. Loftwick stops being a safe place to work. A genial Council deputy arrives with a bribe and a warning; a thief comes for the evidence in the night; and Caelith lays three ways east on the table and asks the party to choose how they want to travel — fast and blind, slow and protected, or quiet and deniable. They ride out at dawn. Hardby is six days off. Rel Astra is further, and closer than it looks.

How to read the rest of the Phase 2 journals

This journal — Phase 2 — Start Here — is your orientation. It tells you what the chapter is, what is actually happening behind it, the five truths the party must leave with, and the short form of the Pressure Clock.

The companion journal — Phase 2 — Quick Reference (GM) — is the at-the-table card. It carries the full Pressure Clock segment table, the “if they wander” recovery moves, the “things you never name” guardrails, the hand-off matrix to Phase 3, the cast list, and the full NPC knowledge matrix plus the maps appendix. Open it during play.

The Movement journals — The Morning Review, Wick Lane / Tannerway investigations, Interruptions (Moll & Sarth), and Three Roads East — are the scene scripts themselves. Read them in order before running.

What Is Actually Happening (for your eyes only)

This page is GM-only. The party never reads it. It is the ground-truth of what is actually happening in Loftwick during Phase 2 — the players earn it a piece at a time, and some of it they never get.

Layer 1 — Surface (what the party can prove)

A Rel Astran banking house — Cindren & Vhal, Bonded Factors, with a grand counting house in Rel Astra, a working branch in Hardby, and a single sleepy notary's office here in Loftwick — has spent at least four years laundering paperwork for a client whose name never appears. One of the firm's services to that client is a standing contract on Haskur Vandrell: whenever he falls, a pre-laid contingency yanks him to a safe house and the firm re-files him under a new name. They have done it twice before. The party's fight was the third.

To keep that machine quiet, the firm has been spending money in Loftwick. They paid a Treasury bonds clerk named Merro Gilst for four years to look the other way — and the moment Haskur's escrow was drawn down and Gilst became a man who could notice, they had a contract physician named Reln Pollow certify a tidy heart-failure on a man who had in fact drunk a spiked cup of tea. And a grain factor named Tamsin Moraven, who had been pulling at the firm's false harvest-receipts, walked out of her counting house eleven days ago and did not come back. She is alive. She is being held in Hardby. They have not yet decided what to do with her.

What the party can prove is the firm, the dead clerk, the missing woman, and the single hard fact that ties them together: someone at Cindren & Vhal's Hardby branch personally underwrote Haskur Vandrell six weeks before the party ever met him. The hand is in Hardby. That is where the road goes next.

Layer 2 — Above C&V (the “E.” layer)

  • C&V is not the principal. Someone above C&V is paying the firm to do this work. That someone signs only with the initial “E.”
  • “E.” countersigned three of the forty-one payments to Gilst — the three larger payments, irregularly timed. “E.” also appears once on the initials-list in the back of Gilst's hidden receipts book.
  • The party can find “E.” once. They cannot identify “E.” in Phase 2. No Yeomanry register lists a relevant figure who initials as “E.” Caelith does not recognise the initial. Trina does not recognise the initial.
  • “E.” is the campaign-long whisper. Phase 2 plants it. Phase 4 echoes it in Rel Astra's marginalia. Phase 6 thickens it. Phase 8 records the same mark on under-road registry walls.

    GM: the name Eclavdra of House Eilserv is the answer; do not breathe it across the table until late campaign at earliest.

Layer 3 — The deep craft (the “peculiar” layer)

  • The lacquered scroll tube (evidence #4) is not surface work. The lacquer-laying, the inlay, and the abjuration glyph are all unmistakably elven craftsmanship in a tradition no Yeomanry sage can place.

  • GM-only: it is Drow work, of a tradition kept alive only in Erelhei-Cinlu, far below the world the Yeomanry knows. In this world Drow are an ancient legend no one alive remembers; the Underdark is not a word that exists at this table. The most accurate identification anyone surface-side can offer is, “elven, but peculiar.” Do not name Drow, Eilserv, Erelhei-Cinlu, or the Underdark under any roll.

  • This is the only direct hint in Phase 2 that the conspiracy reaches below the surface world entirely. Phase 8 and Phase 9 confirm the under-road systems that this deep layer administers.
  • If a PC casts legend lore or similar, the response is a long fragmentary verse about a people who walked downward and were forgotten, which the caster cannot place. The peculiarity is the entire reveal in Phase 2.

What is in motion during Phase 2 (offstage)

While the party investigates, the following are happening that they do not see:

  1. Hesren Vesh's couriers. The moment the party shows their hand at the Wick Lane satellite, Vesh sends a sealed message to C&V Hardby. Hardby responds by escalating with Merev Sarth.
  2. Arthen Moll's faction prepares. Two days into the party's inquiry, Moll's office begins drafting the Civic Reconciliation Closure motion. They file it at Pressure Clock segment 5.
  3. Pollow's nerves. Pollow knows he is the next to be disposed of. He is packing on his own initiative even if the party never visits him. If unwarned, the conspirators reach him on the third day after the party identifies Gilst's death.
  4. Tamsin Moraven is alive in Hardby. Held in a sealed room above a Cindren & Vhal-owned warehouse. She has not been harmed. They have not decided what to do with her. Phase 3 resolves her fate.
  5. Sarth's broker accepts the retrieval contract. A man known to Sarth as “Tarsh” (Hardby intermediary) communicates the contract to her via a numbered dead drop. Sarth never meets him.
  6. A clerk in the Audit Hall — not yet named — learns that the party is in the Strong Room and quietly reports up the chain. This is the “inventory tampering” tipoff at Pressure Clock segment 1. Caelith will hunt this clerk down later; Phase 2 does not name them.
  7. The Vandrell letter of credit's value is settled. The 6,000 gp escrow drawn down on the night the party fought Haskur was credited to a numbered Cindren & Vhal Rel Astra account. Phase 4 connects that account to a specific senior partner.

What the conspirators expect

The conspirators expect the party to either accept Moll's bribe or be administratively contained by the Council. They do not expect the party to keep moving when both fail. The Sarth retrieval is a low-confidence backstop. From the conspirators' perspective, Phase 2 is the last chance they have to contain the matter inside the Yeomanry. After the party crosses the border, the matter becomes someone else's problem — specifically, Sereth's, in Rel Astra.

Deep seed — the Hardby 580 barmaid (long-game foreshadowing)

The barmaid who put a hand on Haskur's chest in Hardby in 580 CY and vanished with him is named in Caelith's speech as a loose end he never identified. Canon, never named at the table: she was not a witness but a hand — one of “E.”’s own, who performed Haskur's 580 recovery and was herself drawn down to the under-road afterward. She cannot be found in Loftwick, Hardby, or any port; a party who files her name is recording an early sighting of the route's recovery mechanism. She resurfaces only much later and far below, as a drow slave — a deep-future payoff, not an Act I lead. If the party asks Caelith about her at any point, his honest answer is: “She was, when I went looking, also not there.” Leave it at that.

The Shape of the Chapter

Three movements, across roughly three in-fiction days and two to four sessions. This page expands what the Before You Run This overview sketched; use it as the GM's map of the whole chapter.

Movement One — The Table

The first movement is the table. The party comes down to breakfast and finds Haskur's possessions laid out as evidence in daylight; Caelith tells them who he really is and what he wants; and the morning's reading turns a handful of objects into three names and two streets to walk to.

Two scenes carry it:

  • Scene 1 — The Morning Review in the Refectory of the Little Palace. Seven objects on numbered linen, each rewarding a closer look. The room delivers the names Vandrell, Moraven, Gilst via the ledger fragment.
  • Scene 2 — Behind the Library Wall, the Records Alcove. The party turns three bare names into two streets they can walk to: Wick Lane (the firm, the dead clerk, the physician) and Tannerway (the missing factor, her husband, the watch sergeant).

Movement Two — The City

The second movement is the city. The party works two neighborhoods at their own pace. Let them go in whatever order they like, and let them spend a day in halves rather than splitting the party across the city (if they insist on splitting, call them back together for a working lunch at the Little Palace).

  • Scene 3 — Wick Lane is the dense one: paper-trade row, three doors within sight of one another (Vesh at the firm's green door; Reith and Gilst's rooms above the stationer's; Pollow's surgery two doors down) and a watcher on the corner who turns the street into a footchase.
  • Scene 3d — The Sparrow's Run: the chapter's one true footrace. Anla “Sparrow” Coyle is meant to get away cleanly because she's escaping across her own ground; the fold drops at the Loft Steps regardless, so the clue pays out on a win, a loss, or a draw.
  • Scene 4 — Tannerway is the human one: Doril Veth at the counting house, Vellin Moraven at home with the Sevenday note, Sergeant Halir Anwic at the watch house. Nothing here runs and nothing is a trap. The verb that matters is delayed, not settled: Tamsin is alive and on a clock.
  • Interlude — The Mentor. The Trina-and-Alicia thread is woven through this movement; ration it one beat per session.

Movement Three — The Squeeze and the Road

The third movement is the squeeze and the road. Loftwick stops being a safe place to work. A genial Council deputy arrives with a bribe and a warning; a thief comes for the evidence in the night; and Caelith lays three ways east on the table and asks the party to choose how they want to travel — fast and blind, slow and protected, or quiet and deniable.

  • Scene 5 — The Friendly Wall (Arthen Moll). The bribe is real (1,200 gp each) and the threat is institutional, not violent. Moll is the wall the soft-pressure faction puts up before things get loud.
  • Scene 6 — The Night Visitor (Merev Sarth). The conspirators try the other kind of pressure. The chest holds decoys; the tunnel is watched. Sarth is on a retrieval contract, paid extra not to leave bodies, and she yields at 30 HP. Run her as a kill-the-party encounter and you've misread the scene.
  • Scene 7 — Three Roads East. Caelith lays three folios on the table. “I can give you authority, cover, or distance. I cannot give you all three.” The Official Delegation, the Commercial Cover (Caelith's quiet recommendation), the Quiet Pursuit. They ride out at dawn.

Time budget

Phase 2 plays in 2–4 sessions. The minimum-viable run is 2 sessions if the party drives directly through. A full run with all optional scenes (Gilst's rooms in full, the Pollow confrontation, the Sparrow's Run, Tannerway in full, the Moll and Sarth interruptions, and all the Trina & Alicia mentorship beats) runs 4 sessions comfortably.

The Five Truths

You can let almost anything in this chapter go sideways except these. The party should leave Loftwick believing, in their bones:

  1. Haskur survived by design, not luck. Someone paid, in advance, to pull him back from death.
  2. He is a node in a network, not a lone agent — a name the system moves around like an account.
  3. Cindren & Vhal sit at the center, and the living hand is in Hardby.
  4. Loftwick will not let them finish this here. There is pressure, political and criminal, to make them stop.
  5. The road runs Loftwick → Hardby → Rel Astra, and it must be taken now, with urgency.

Every one of these is carried by at least three different clues in the chapter, so no single missed roll or skipped room can cost the party the truth. If you reach the final scene and one of the five hasn't landed, the full Quick Reference journal tells you which late line supplies it.

You should arrive at the gate with all five in hand and the party arguing about how to go east, not whether.

How to use this at the table

Tick these off as they land. Each is carried by several clues, so a missed roll never costs the party a truth — but if you reach Scene 7 and one is thin, use one of the backstop lines from the Quick Reference's Five Truths (Full) table.

The most common omissions in the first run are:

  • Truth 1 (by design) — if the party skipped most of the Morning Review, Caelith's speech about the two prior “deaths” (Onnwal 575, Hardby 580) does all the work alone. Lean on it.
  • Truth 3 (Hardby is the hand) — if Gilst's rooms went un-searched, Vesh's four words “reads like Hardby work” and the chase fold's east on the Sevenday cart together cover it; if both of those also missed, Caelith reads Tamsin's last three queries aloud.
  • Truth 5 (the road, now) — if the party hasn't felt urgency, Trina supplies it: “The thing you are looking for is not in Loftwick. I will push where Caelith will not.”

The other two truths land almost on their own — Truth 2 (node in a network) comes from the very existence of a ledger fragment with three names and no sums; Truth 4 (Loftwick won't let them finish) is delivered by the Moll and Sarth interruptions in Movement Three.

The Heartbeat — the Pressure Clock

This chapter runs on a clock, and you should put it on the table where everyone can see it: six segments on a notecard, filled in one at a time. It is not a doom timer. It is the sound of the conspiracy noticing the party — and every time it ticks, something happens in the fiction that hands the party a new thing to react to. A clerk reports that the evidence was disturbed in the night. A note arrives warning their host to throw them out. The street starts gossiping. A witness goes cold.

When to advance

You advance it when the party does something the conspiracy can see from outside the building — showing their hand at the firm's office, rattling the physician, making a public spectacle — and when a whole day passes with nothing accomplished. You do not advance it for honest investigation done quietly; re-reading the evidence at the breakfast table costs nothing.

Target curve

Aim to reach the final scene with the clock at three or four. That's pressure landing on the party without ever having stopped them.

End of sessionTarget clock
Session 11–2
Session 22–3
Session 3 (final)3–4

The single rule that matters

You can run the whole chapter knowing only this:

When in doubt, let the clock tick, and read what it does out loud.

The full segment-by-segment table, the “if it runs hot” and “if it runs cold” recovery moves, and the what to telegraph each segment reference are all on the @UUID[Compendium.blooming-rot-2.phase-2-journals.JournalEntry.BR2P2QRefJournal.JournalEntryPage.BR2P2QrPgClock]{Pressure Clock in Full} page of the companion @UUID[Compendium.blooming-rot-2.phase-2-journals.JournalEntry.BR2P2QRefJournal]{Phase 2 — Quick Reference (GM)} journal. Open that at the table; this page is the one-paragraph briefing.

GM: track the clock openly. Players should never be ambushed by a clock advance. They should feel its weight. Every tick is a gift of momentum, not a punishment.
